## Top 5 Trends and Innovations In Vertical Farming

Vertical farming is experiencing a wave of innovation, and it's arriving just when we need it most. With global food systems under increasing pressure from climate change, urbanization, and supply chain disruptions, we're rethinking how and where we grow our food.

Vertical farming—a method of growing crops indoors in [stacked layers](https://commercial.justvertical.com/pages/free-standing-grow-rack)—offers a more sustainable, resilient, and scalable solution. Paired with [hydroponics](https://agriculture.canada.ca/en/science/story-agricultural-science/scientific-achievements-agriculture/what-hydroponics), this approach opens up year-round food production almost anywhere, from the heart of major cities to the edges of remote communities. What's more is that new technologies such as AI, automation, and precision lighting are taking indoor farming to the next level, making it smarter, faster, and more adaptable than ever before.

In this post, we'll explore the top five trends driving the future of vertical farming, complete with real-world examples and insights. Whether you're just starting to explore indoor growing or looking to optimize your setup, this guide will help you understand what's possible—and what's coming next.

If you're new to vertical farming and or hydroponics, take a look at our [Definitive Guide](https://commercial.justvertical.com/blogs/learning/what-is-vertical-farming-the-definitive-guide) before digging into this one!

![A modern vertical grow rack illuminated by LED lights.](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/1908/8333/files/IMAGE_1_Alt_Text_A_modern_vertical_grow_rack_illuminated_by_LED_lights..png?v=1753801855)

## Table of Contents

## AI, Automation, and Robotics

**How are AI, automation, and robotics being used in vertical farms?**

AI, automation, and robotics in vertical farming are changing the way we grow food. These technologies monitor crops, control growing conditions, and handle routine tasks—often in real-time. As a result, farms are becoming smarter, faster, and more productive.

For example:

These [advancements in vertical farming](https://nuleaffarms.ca/blog/ai-and-the-future-of-indoor-farming-how-automation-and-data-is-helping-growers-boost-profitability-and-feed-the-future/) make indoor farms more reliable and efficient. In traditional farming, everything must be done manually. However, with indoor farming innovations like automation, machines handle repetitive tasks, allowing humans to focus on cultivating better crops.

A great example of this in action is [Dyson's](https://dysonfarming.com/strawberries/) newly launched hybrid strawberry farm. This indoor farming facility uses a blend of hydroponics, advanced robotics, and AI to grow strawberries in high-density racks, allowing them to produce more food with less space. Robots move along rails to monitor plant health, deliver nutrients, and manage the growing environment, all while collecting valuable data to optimize crop performance. What makes this farm especially interesting is that it blends the best of both worlds: natural sunlight through glass ceilings and the precision of AI-driven indoor farming. It's a clear sign that with the right tools, we can grow fresh, healthy food year-round, in the heart of cities or remote communities.

Our trusted partner in [NuLeaf farms](https://nuleaffarms.ca/) is also another great example of a company excelling in automation with indoor farming. Through their leaf system, they've been able to develop and expand the automation in either pre-existing farm operations, or new farm operations just beginning their growing journey.

## Advanced Lighting Systems

**How are advanced lighting systems transforming vertical farming?**

Advanced lighting systems in vertical farming are helping farmers produce healthier crops while using less energy. These smart systems use tunable [LED lights](https://nuleaffarms.ca/blog/the-benefits-of-properly-designed-led-lighting-systems/) that adjust the light spectrum and intensity based on a plant's stage of growth. As a result, plants get exactly the kind of light they need—no more, no less.

Many vertical farms now connect their lighting systems to sensors and automation software. These tools monitor plant growth and make real-time changes to light levels. For instance, lights can be dimmed or brightened depending on plant needs or energy usage goals. This kind of control is a game-changer for indoor farming innovations.

To summarize, here's how smart LED lighting improves vertical farming:

Discover how [#LEDLighting](https://twitter.com/hashtag/LEDLighting?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) impacts red and green microgreens and why spectrum control is essential for optimal growth.<https://t.co/aWPhOrS1wy>[#LEDLighting](https://twitter.com/hashtag/LEDLighting?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) [#LEDGrowLights](https://twitter.com/hashtag/LEDGrowLights?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) [#GROW3](https://twitter.com/hashtag/GROW3?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) [#GrowLights](https://twitter.com/hashtag/GrowLights?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) [pic.twitter.com/OnxBkRs1dk](https://t.co/OnxBkRs1dk)

Some advanced LED systems in vertical farming now go a step further by [simulating natural sunrise and sunset cycles](https://www.atophort.com/news/sunrise-sunset-simulation-indoor-crops-need-know.html#:~:text=How%20sunrise%20and%20sunset%20stimulation,yields%20and%20better%20crop%20quality.). Instead of switching on at full brightness, these lights gradually ramp up in the morning and dim down in the evening, mimicking the way natural light behaves outdoors. This gentle transition helps reduce stress on the plants, supporting more consistent growth and improving overall crop quality. For growers focused on premium produce, this small but powerful adjustment in lighting can make a noticeable difference.

## Modular and Containerized Farms

**What makes modular and containerized farms so effective?**

Modular and [containerized farms](https://commercial.justvertical.com/blogs/learning/how-shipping-container-farms-are-changing-farming) are a key advancement in vertical farming because they offer speed, flexibility, and scalability. Built using prefabricated containers or stackable units, these farms can be placed in tight urban spaces, remote areas, or even disaster zones. As a result, more communities can grow fresh, local food without needing traditional farmland.

These indoor farming innovations are fully equipped with hydroponics, advanced lighting systems, and climate controls. Moreover, operators can easily scale production by adding more units, making them ideal for fast-growing operations.

Here's why modular and containerized farms are growing in popularity:

![Technicians working just outside of a retrofitted shipping container farm.](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/1908/8333/files/IMAGE_2_Alt_Text_Technicians_working_just_outside_of_a_retrofitted_shipping_container_farm..png?v=1753801855)

These vertical farming systems are being adopted across many industries. You'll find them in schools, food banks, [restaurants](https://commercial.justvertical.com/blogs/learning/vertical-farming-for-restaurants-a-guide-to-fresh-local-and-sustainable-produce), hospitals, and even in [humanitarian relief efforts](https://commercial.justvertical.com/blogs/learning/our-ceos-perspective-indoor-farming-for-refugees-in-moldova). Because they're easy to set up and manage, they're also a popular option for beginners and small organizations looking to grow food year-round.

One standout example of our work at Just Vertical is a [container farm built at Seneca College](https://grow.justvertical.com/hubfs/Just-Vertical-Case-Studies-JULY-2024-Seneca-College.pdf?hsCtaAttrib=173723669292)—a fully equipped unit that serves as both a hands-on teaching tool and a reliable source of fresh food. The compact farm grows produce year-round using hydroponics, advanced lighting, and climate control systems—all neatly housed within a standard shipping container. What makes this project particularly impactful is how seamlessly it fits into the college's sustainability and education programs. Students gain real-world experience with cutting-edge farming technology, while the farm itself helps support local food security.

[A post shared by Sustainable Seneca (@sustainableseneca)](https://www.instagram.com/reel/C3nf0eFrF4a/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading)

## Aquaponics and Integrated Systems

**Combining The Power of Fish With Vertical Farming**

[Aquaponics](https://youtube.com/shorts/bH8r54W89WM?si=xlC1Z10IR9sA03iE) and integrated systems are powerful innovations in vertical farming. These setups combine aquaculture (raising fish) with hydroponics (growing plants without soil). As a result, they create a closed-loop system that recycles water and nutrients naturally. This means less waste, less input, and more output.

Aquaponics is gaining traction in vertical farming for several key reasons:

![A diagram of a basic aquaponics system.](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/1908/8333/files/IMAGE_3_Alt_Text_A_diagram_of_a_basic_aquaponics_system..png?v=1753801853)

These [integrated systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0040162523003943?utm_source=chatgpt.com) are particularly helpful in places where water is scarce or land is limited. In addition, farmers can earn income from both the plants and the fish, increasing profitability with what's often called a dual crop model.

A recent innovation in this space is the SKYGROW Vertical Aquaponic System by [Skytech](https://www.skytechen.com/). Built with cities in mind, SKYGROW takes the idea of aquaponics and brings it to the next level—literally. What really stands out is how easy it is to manage: the system is fully automated, constantly checking water quality and nutrient levels so you don't have to. It's also modular, meaning it can grow with your needs, whether you're starting small or scaling up. With its smart design and low-waste approach, SKYGROW shows just how accessible and efficient aquaponics can be in modern urban farming.

Aquaponics isn't just sustainable—it's scalable, profitable, and packed with potential.

## Sustainability and ESG

**How Vertical Farming and Sustainability Go Hand-In-Hand**

Sustainability is only one part of the picture. Vertical farming also supports strong **[Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG)](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/esg-investments-on-sustainable-greenhouse-technology)** outcomes. As companies, governments, and organizations come under growing pressure to prove their impact, vertical farming offers a transparent, measurable way to contribute.

Socially, vertical farms create local jobs, improve access to fresh food in underserved areas, and support community resilience. From food banks to school cafeterias, [they can be built to serve real needs](https://commercial.justvertical.com/blogs/learning/driving-social-impact-with-indoor-farming) where it matters most.

From an ESG lens, vertical farming contributes to multiple **[UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)](https://sdgs.un.org/goals)**, including:

Investing in vertical farming isn't just [good for the environment](https://nuleaffarms.ca/blog/sustainable-farming-practices-the-environmental-impact-of-commercial-vertical-farming/)—it's a clear path to delivering impact, building a future-ready business, and meeting global sustainability commitments head-on.

### FAQ: Your Questions Answered

**1. What are the main trends in vertical farming?**  
The leading trends include AI and automation, smart LED lighting, modular systems, aquaponics, and sustainability. Each one is reshaping how farms operate today.

**2. Is vertical farming a good investment?**  
Yes. As technology improves and demand rises, vertical farms are becoming more cost-effective and profitable, especially in urban and remote areas.

**3. What's the difference between hydroponics and aquaponics?**  
Hydroponics uses a nutrient solution; aquaponics adds fish into the mix to create a closed-loop, symbiotic system that benefits both plants and fish.

**4. Can vertical farming work in cold or remote climates?**  
Containerized and indoor farms are climate-controlled, meaning they can function year-round in any location.

**5. Why are ESG and sustainability such big topics in farming?**  
Traditional agriculture uses massive amounts of land, water, and energy. Vertical farming minimizes all three, aligning with global goals for environmental responsibility.

## What's Next?

**The Future of Vertical Farming**

The [future of vertical farming](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/future-of-vertical-farming#:~:text=By%20combining%20smart%20design%20with,while%20using%20significantly%20less%20water.) is all about smart integration. As biotech, AI, and modular growing systems advance, vertical farms are becoming more scalable, efficient, and adaptable. These systems are now being built into grocery stores, restaurant basements, rooftop patios, and mixed-use developments, proving they can thrive in the places we already live, work, and gather.

It's clear that vertical farming is much more than just a trend—it's a complete shift in how we think about food. Urban planners and developers are starting to see vertical farms as part of critical infrastructure, alongside EV chargers, solar panels, and smart water systems. By embedding food production into city design, we can reduce food miles, strengthen local economies, and build climate resilience from the ground up.

![A close-up of a youth's hands picking lettuce from a greenwall.](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/1908/8333/files/IMAGE_4_Alt_Text_A_close-up_of_a_youth_s_hands_picking_lettuce_from_a_greenwall..png?v=1753801855)

At Just Vertical, we see vertical farming as more than a tool—it's a movement. One that has the potential to localize food systems, increase access to healthy produce, and build a more sustainable future for all. Whether you're a city builder, educator, entrepreneur, or community advocate, [we're here to help you bring that vision to life.](https://commercial.justvertical.com/pages/getting-started-with-indoor-farming)

Cities are heavily reliant on imported food to survive. Be it from the countryside surrounding it or other countries hundreds or even thousands of miles away.

In the latter case, this requires packaging, storing, and transportation of fresh food in such a way that it arrives at its final destination unspoiled and almost as good as the moment it was picked. A vast, seemingly insane investment in labor and energy could be avoided if the food could be grown *in* the city. But, with real estate usually at a premium, how could this be possible?

Enter a system called [vertical farming](https://interestingengineering.com/new-projects-combine-vertical-farming-with-affordable-housing).

This revolutionary form of agriculture is still in its infancy, but its potential is truly groundbreaking. Let’s find out why.

## What is vertical farming?

[Vertical farming](https://verticalfarminstitute.org/vertical-farming/), as the name suggests, is a form of agriculture that is specifically designed to facilitate agricultural production inside vertical structures like buildings. Large-scale versions tend to consist of a series of vertically stacked surfaces that are usually, though not always, integrated into existing buildings, like office blocks.

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/LBGRM9noO2/vertical-harvest-2.jpg)

[Vertical Harvest](https://verticalharvestfarms.com/press-media/#photos)

They can also be created inside repurposed warehouses, used shipping containers, greenhouses, or other buildings normally unsuitable for large-scale farming.

You can also make small, domestic-scale ones in your own home or garden on a [shoestring budget](https://www.lowtechmagazine.com/2010/03/make-your-own-lowtech-vertical-farm.html).

For this reason, vertical farming can be performed in places that traditionally lack the enormous amounts of open space needed for growing crops — like in metropolitan areas.

[Vertical farming](https://interestingengineering.com/vertical-farming-revolution-coming-store-near-you) is a revolutionary and arguably more sustainable method of farming for several reasons. For example, it tends to require much less water than regular farming — by some estimates, up to 95% less.

This is because a proportion of the water used can be recycled and reused. Additionally, less water is lost to evaporation.

It also takes up less space (especially the ground footprint) and has little to no impact on local natural soils (apart from the ground surface right below). According to the [Vertical Farming Institute](https://verticalfarminstitute.org/vertical-farming/), every square meter (10.76 sq ft) of floor space given to vertical farming produces approximately the same amount of vegetable crops as 50 square meters (538 sq ft) of conventionally worked farmland.

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-domestic.jpg)

[Blaine O’Neill/Flickr](https://flickr.com/photos/blaineo/2984552937/)

Generally speaking, vertical farms can be operated without the use of pesticides and herbicides that are potentially very damaging to the environment. Because of the controlled conditions provided by vertical farms, all-year-round cultivation of crops is also usually made possible.

Vertical farms are also seen as a great way to deal with an urban phenomenon called “[food deserts](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/what-are-food-deserts)“. This refers to heavily populated areas that lack access to fresh foods like fruits and vegetables. Because vertical farms can generally be built on a small footprint (or inside existing structures), they can improve easy access to fresh food, which would not need to be shipped from distant farms. Fresh food can be sourced locally rather than being shipped in and stored before consumption.

## How does vertical farming work?

Vertical farms tend to consist of one of several models, ranging from stacked wooden shelves on garden patios to warehouses and greenhouses that are able to produce enough food for entire communities to retrofitted facilities in buildings like skyscrapers.

Some, like those built by [Eden Green Technology](https://www.edengreen.com/), include specially designed towers with stacked plant cups. For larger, purpose-built vertical farms, hydroponic systems are often employed to help control the indoor climate all year round.

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-hydroponics.jpg)

[Bright Agrotech/Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hydroponic_vertical_farm.jpg)

Other aspects of vertical farms can also be regulated, including automated control of temperature, light, and humidity. A high level of control is usually vital for the farm’s success, as failure to maintain specific conditions can lead to the loss of entire crops, similar to how events such as droughts and floods impact traditional farms.

There are many other examples, and we’ll briefly cover a few notable ones.

## How to start vertical farming, or how can you invest in vertical farming?

If you are interested in **investing in vertical farming** or starting your own vertical farm, you have a few options, but these will also depend on your aspirations. For domestic-scale “off the grid” kind of strategies, simple vertical farms can be created using old pallets or other custom-made solutions.

You can also buy customized or standardized, off-the-shelf vertical farm solutions too. We have included a few examples later on in this article.

For larger-scale vertical farming options, you can either directly invest in existing vertical farms (either public or private) or raise enough capital to set up your own. This will, obviously, require large amounts of capital, but the potential for such solutions is predicted to rise dramatically over the coming years.

Some companies (also listed below) offer larger-scale pre-packaged vertical farming solutions that can be quickly scaled up, depending on needs. Many of these solutions can also be combined with more traditional farming techniques.

## What are some examples of vertical farming?

Now that we’ve covered some of the basics, let’s look at some interesting examples of currently available **vertical farming techniques**. This list is far from exhaustive and is in no particular order.

## **1. Hydroponics – growing plants without soil**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/hydroponics.jpg)

[Oregon State University/Flickr](https://www.flickr.com/photos/oregonstateuniversity/33313395775)

**Hydroponics** is a common system of growing plants used in most forms of vertical farming, and it is slowly but steadily gaining importance. It involves the growth of plants in [solutions of nutrients](https://www.verticalroots.com/the-what-and-why-of-hydroponic-farming/) rather than soil — as in traditional farming.

In this **vertical farming technique**, the roots of the plants are submerged in a nutrient-rich solution which is frequently circulated and monitored.

## **2. Aeroponics – growing plants with no soil and very little water**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/Aeroponics_Tomato_and_Lettuce.jpg)

[MyAeroponics/Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aeroponics_Tomato_and_Lettuce.jpg)

**Aeroponics** is another method used in vertical farming. First [proposed in the early-20th century](https://modernfarmer.com/2018/07/how-does-aeroponics-work/), the technique was refined by NASA in the 1990s when it was looking for efficient techniques to grow plants in space.

This method involves growing plants in an air or mist environment with no soil and very little water. In **aeroponics**, seeds or seedlings are “planted” in pieces of foam stuffed into tiny pots, which are exposed to light on one end and nutrient mist on the other. The foam holds the stem and root mass in place as the plants grow.

**Aeroponics** is considered one of the most efficient methods of vertical farming, as it uses over [90% less water](https://www.towerfarms.com/content/dam/towerfarm/workshop-resources/tower-farms-signage.pdf) than even the most efficient **hydroponics** systems. And since the nutrients are held in the water, they get recycled, too. It has also been observed that the plants that are grown this way tend to take up [more vitamins and minerals](https://www.nasa.gov/vision/earth/technologies/aeroponic_plants.html), which may make the plants healthier and more nutritious. The extra oxygen the plants are exposed to also results in faster growth.

## **3. Aquaponics – growing fish and plants together in one place**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/aquaponics.jpg)

[Kate Field/Flickr](https://www.flickr.com/photos/gatewayhorticulture/6852357005/in/album-72157629260128361/)

Another technique used in vertical farms is **aquaponics**. In this system, fish grow in indoor ponds and produce nutrient-rich waste, which is a [food source for the plants](https://extension.oregonstate.edu/news/transform-fish-waste-food-aquaponics-garden) grown in vertical farms.

The plants, in return, purify and filter the wastewater, which is recycled directly back into the fish ponds.

Along with fish and plants, microbes also play an important role in converting fish waste products into useful nutrients for plants.

## **4. Lokal – growing food in situ**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/lokal-ikea.jpg)

[Space10](https://space10.com/project/lokal/)

Another interesting **vertical farming innovation** is a system called Lokal. Developed in collaboration with IKEA’s Space 10 innovation lab, [Lokal is a sort of pop-up farming system](https://space10.com/project/lokal/). Crops are grown hydroponically and primarily rely on artificial light, like LEDs, rather than natural sunlight.

According to the designers of Lokal, their system allows plants to grow three times faster than traditional gardens. In the demonstration model, a salad bar was set up in front of the farm so the food could be served immediately.

In the future, it is hoped to integrate sensors and machine learning into the system to enable it to connect to and be controlled by devices like Google Home.

## **5. AeroFarms – the smart vertical farming innovation**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/aerofarms.jpg)

[AeroFarms](http://aerofarms.com/)

First founded in 2004, a [company called AeroFarms](https://www.aerofarms.com/how-we-grow/) is making waves in the field of vertical farming. Using a modified form of **aeroponics**, they have combined genetics, engineering, food safety, data science, and nutrition in developing their products.

According to AeroFarms, “our commercial farms are optimized for year-round production, no matter the season or weather, and we have grown over 550 different varieties of plants, including leafy greens, berries, tomatoes, and more.”

AeroFarms aims to transform the whole system of agriculture by building and making farms that are environmentally responsible. They are building farms around the world to make sure there is a local production of food that is nutritious, safe, sustainable, and delicious.

In short, they want to grow more crops in less space to bring about a food revolution.

## **6. Plantscapers – a building that provides food for its occupants**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/Plantagon-world-food-building.jpg)

[Plantagon](http://www.plantagon.com)

A Swedish food tech company called [Plantagon](http://www.plantagon.com/) has come up with a creative solution that allows office spaces and other high-rise buildings to help grow food to help feed its occupants. Their system is partly based on the concept of vertical greenhouses developed by Åke Olsson — from whom they bought the intellectual property rights in 2008.

Olsson developed a rack transport system that gradually moved the planting boxes from the floor to the ceiling of a vertical greenhouse, thus requiring no artificial light. This system can be installed in new builds or retrofitted to existing buildings.

At present, the company is currently attempting to showcase its vertical farming system in a purpose-built building in [Linköping, Sweden](http://www.plantagon.com/about/brand/history/).

It is hoped that it will produce enough food to feed almost 5000 people and includes high-level automation that will be employed for the maintenance and harvest of plants to keep the costs very low.

Moreover, everything from sunlight, temperature, and nutrition, to air quality will be measured through autonomous and controlled systems.

If the concept becomes a success, more countries, such as Singapore, Hong Kong, the United States, and others, have plans to adopt it too.

## **7. VertiCrop** **– a sustainable farming technique for urban areas**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-verticrop.jpg)

[VertiCrop](http://www.verticrop.com/)

[VertiCrop](http://www.verticrop.com/) is a proprietary agriculture technique that was been described as one of the “[World’s Greatest Inventions](http://content.time.com/time/specials/packages/article/0,28804,1934027_1934003_1933961,00.html)” by TIME Magazine in 2009. This patent-pending technology was designed and developed so that food could be grown naturally in the environment of bustling urban areas.

This **vertical farming technique** provides up to twenty times the yield of standard field crops while simultaneously using only 8% of the water that is usually needed for soil farming.

The vertical farming innovation works on a suspended tray configuration that is unique in itself and moves on a conveyor system. VertiCrop offers optimal exposure to both artificial and natural light in addition to nutrients that are precisely measured for every plant.

It has been designed in such a way that it can promote the healthy growth of crops in controlled and closed-loop environments. Furthermore, it entirely gets rid of the need for using harmful herbicides and pesticides and maximizes food value, nutrition, and above all, taste.

## **8. Modular farms – produce fresh plants virtually anywhere in the world**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-modular-farms.jpg)

[modularfarms](https://www.modularfarms.com.au/)

Developed by a company called [ModularFarms](https://www.modularfarms.com.au/), this vertical farming solution is an entirely indoor system that has the ability to produce healthy and fresh plants virtually in any climate and anywhere in the world.

Not only that, but it provides a level of customization that provides it with endless application possibilities. Being modular, this vertical farming solution can be scaled rapidly in size and the types of crops it can grow.

Their vertical farming system uses purpose-built steel containers (not modified shipping containers) that can be installed to supplement existing farms, remote isolated communities, or dense urban centers. [Each container](https://www.modularfarms.com.au/primary-module-2/) is effectively self-sufficient and includes everything from lighting, water supply and treatment, environmental control, and everything else the plants need — just hook it up to the power and water supply.

## **9. Cubic farming systems – the next-gen in sustainable farming**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-cubicfarms.jpg)

[cubicfarms](https://cubicfarms.com/fresh-produce/)

Another interesting development in vertical farming is something called [CubicFarms](https://cubicfarms.com/fresh-produce/). This fully automated, environmentally-controlled system uses specialized trays that can be moved in a “v-shape” motion to ensure all sides of the plant receive just the right amount of light to maximize their growth.

The system is based on the concept of **hydroponics**, with the addition of sophisticated water, nutrient, light, and environmental control systems, all contained within specially designed steel containers. Each installation tends to be custom-built and is shipped in an effectively complete state.

## **10. ZipGrow – vertical farming for the modern farmer**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-zipgrow.jpg)

[ZipGrow](https://zipgrow.com/zipfarm/)

Another interesting solution in vertical farming comes from a [company called ZipGrow](https://zipgrow.com/zipfarm/). They have combined the tried and tested use of greenhouses with the principles of vertical farming and **hydroponics** to develop an innovative, low-cost, high-yield method of farming.

Suitable for domestic and industrial scale, ZipGrow’s solutions also offer, in theory, at least all-year-round crop production for its clients, whether they are commercial farmers or homeowners and hobbyists.

The ZipGrow system uses a specially designed rack system with artificial lighting and a specially designed water recirculation system that saves between 90 and 95% of water consumption over more conventional farming techniques.

## **11. Bowery – possibly the most technologically sophisticated commercial indoor farm in the world**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/bowery-farming.jpg)

[boweryfarming/Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/boweryfarming/)

Utilizing **hydroponics**, [Bowery is another company](https://boweryfarming.com/farms/) making waves in the vertical farming community. According to the company, its vertical stacking solution allows users to produce 100 times more in the same space if traditional farming techniques were used.

Bowery’s system uses LEDs and finely controlled air conditions to provide plants with the “ideal balance of nutrients, light, oxygen, and CO2.”

It also uses a closed-loop system to circulate and purify water in order to oxygenate plants and significantly reduce water consumption.

## **12. Skyfarm – a wind-powered vertical farming tower**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/img/iea/rV6n4qnlOX/vertical-farming-skyfarm.jpg)

[Rogers Stirk Harbour + Partners](https://www.rsh-p.com/news/skyfarm-wins-sustainability-award-at-mipim/)

The architectural firm Rogers Stirk Harbour and Partners from London has developed designs for a new, interesting vertical farming concept called [Skyfarm](https://www.rsh-p.com/projects/skyfarm/). The idea is to build a hyperboloid tower using different farming techniques, including **aquaponics** and traditional soil-based planting methods, to produce crops within high-density urban areas or where land availability is less.

The multi-story building would also partially be built from bamboo to create a rigid circular frame while maximizing the sun exposure on the farm. The tower supports the growth of crops and fishes together through a re-circulating system where the nutrients from fish water are fed to the crops while the plants provide filters for the fish to thrive.

The bottom of the tower would contain market and restaurant spaces to help raise awareness among the public and allow them to enjoy the fruits of the project. On the next level is a large transparent tank consisting of freshwater for farming fish like bass, *tilapia*, and *barramundi*. Above this, plants would be grown using a **hydroponics** system.

Above the hydroponics level, plants would be grown through **aeroponics** using only water mists and no soil. And finally, the top of the tower would have water tanks and turbines to power the whole thing.

This tower is an example of a sustainable solution for growing produce with a short shelf life around the year with easy accessibility for the urban population.

## 13. **Sky Greens – possibly the world’s first hydraulically-driven vertical farm**

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/images/JULY/Sky-Greens.jpg)

[Sky Greens](http://www.skygreens.com)

The Singapore-based company Sky Greens has developed a revolutionary vertical farming system that might also be the world’s first low-carbon, hydraulically driven farm. Vegetables are planted on specially designed growing troughs, mounted on A-shape aluminum frames, that are kept on rotation throughout the day.

These frames can be as high as 30 feet (9 meters) tall, each with 38 tiers of growing troughs, and each capable of accommodating different growing mediums like soil or hydroponics.

The plants at the bottom receive water, while the ones at the top receive sunlight, with all plants constantly cycled throughout the day. This approach, the designers believe, minimizes the use of water, land, and energy over conventional farming techniques.

In addition, the Sky Urban Vertical Farming System is capable of producing, it is claimed, [10 times more yield](http://www.skygreens.com/technology/) compared to traditional farms. While the system is currently used to grow varieties of Asian vegetables, it could conceivably be used to grow all kinds of fruits and veggies.

And that, vertical farming enthusiasts, is your lot for today.

With more and more people living in cities and the pressure to supply good quality fresh food close to the point of consumption, solutions like vertical farming could be the future of farming in many parts of the world.

#### RECOMMENDED ARTICLES

From dense urban settings to more hospitable parts of the world, vertical farming is only just “finding its legs” and is predicted to [be a very big player in the future of agriculture](https://www.google.com/aclk?sa=l&ai=DChcSEwiAytGc7aXwAhUZ03cKHadSAvwYABAAGgJlZg&ae=2&sig=AOD64_1jNDwukzAYCW2aOUCvbXBnh1x5lA&q&adurl&ved=2ahUKEwidxsec7aXwAhVO2KQKHZ4eCi8Q0Qx6BAgDEAE).

SUMMARY:
The Office of Urban Agriculture and Innovative Production (OUAIP) coordinates across USDA agencies to update programs and engage with stakeholders to serve the needs of urban and innovative producers. *   **Urban Agriculture and Innovation Production Grants** initiate or expand efforts of farmers, gardeners, innovative producers, local governments, tribal communities, schools, and other stakeholders to target areas of food access; education; business and start-up costs for new farmers; and development of policies related to zoning and other needs of innovative and urban production. USDA and Department of Energy drafted the Research and Development Potentials in Indoor Agriculture and Sustainable Urban Ecosystems Workshop Report (PDF, 651 KB) which identifies research and development challenges, opportunities, and needs relating to in six major areas: community services, economics, ecosystem services, plant breeding, pest management, and systems engineering.

## Introduction

***Global [vertical farming](https:/agriculture-with-container-farming/)*** refers to the practice of growing crops in vertically stacked layers, using controlled environments, in various locations around the world. This innovative farming method is being adopted globally, from urban centers in the United States and Europe to regions in Asia and the Middle East.

## Table of Contents

## The Global Impact of Vertical Farming

Vertical farming is transforming agriculture by allowing crops to be grown in vertically stacked layers within controlled environments. This innovative approach is being adopted worldwide, providing sustainable solutions for food production in various climates and urban settings.

***“Vertical farming is not just a revolution in agriculture but a necessity to sustainably feed the growing urban population.”***

By using advanced technologies like LED lighting, climate control, and hydroponics or aeroponics, vertical farming ensures consistent crop yields year-round, regardless of external weather conditions.

*“**Vertical farming allows us to produce food with a fraction of the water, no pesticides, and minimal transportation. It’s a win for both people and the planet.”***

This blog explores the global impact of vertical farming, highlighting key regions adopting this technology, the innovative methods being used, and the potential benefits and challenges faced. For example, AeroFarms, the world’s largest indoor vertical farm, has successfully demonstrated how vertical farming can revolutionize urban agriculture.

[**Aero Farms**](https://www.aerofarms.com/)

Join us as we examine the exciting developments in vertical farming and its role in feeding a growing world population.

![Global Vertical Farming](https://agrinextcon.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/agri-2.jpg)

## Key Innovations in Global Vertical Farming

### Hydroponics and Aeroponics:

These soilless farming techniques use nutrient-rich water solutions (hydroponics) or mist (aeroponics) to nourish plants. They minimize water use and eliminate the need for soil, reducing pests and diseases.

### LED Lighting:

 Advances in LED technology have enabled precise control over light spectra, improving photosynthesis efficiency. This allows crops to grow faster and yields to increase while reducing energy consumption.

### Automation and AI:

Automation in vertical farms includes robotic systems for planting, tending, and harvesting crops. AI algorithms optimize environmental conditions and resource use, enhancing productivity and reducing labour costs.

### Energy Efficiency:

Integrating renewable energy sources, such as solar panels and wind turbines, helps vertical farms reduce their carbon footprint. Energy recovery systems and efficient HVAC systems also contribute to sustainability.

### Data Analytics and IoT:

Internet of Things (IoT) devices and data analytics provide real-time monitoring and control over growing conditions. This leads to more precise farming practices and better crop management.

## Global Trends in Vertical Farming

### Urban Agriculture:

With increasing urbanization, cities worldwide are becoming hubs for vertical farming. Urban farms reduce the need for transportation, ensuring fresher produce and lower carbon emissions.

### Investment and Funding:

Venture capital and governmental support are driving the expansion of vertical farming startups globally. Significant investments are being made in technology development and infrastructure.

A prominent name in investing in global vertical farming is **SoftBank Vision Fund.** SoftBank has made significant investments in companies like **Plenty,** a leading vertical farming startup. Another notable investor is **Jeff Bezos,** who has also invested in **Plenty through Bezos Expeditions.** These investors are helping to drive innovation and growth in the vertical farming industry worldwide.

### Diversification of Crops:

Initially focused on leafy greens and herbs, vertical farms are now experimenting with a broader range of crops, including **strawberries, tomatoes, and even staple crops like wheat and rice.**

### Sustainability Initiatives:

Vertical farming aligns with global sustainability goals. It offers solutions to food security challenges, reduces the environmental impact of agriculture, and promotes sustainable urban development.

### Community and Educational Outreach:

Many vertical farms engage with local communities and schools, providing education on sustainable farming practices and healthy eating.

**An example of community and educational outreach in vertical farming is the work done by AeroFarms. They engage in several initiatives, including:**

**Community Workshops and Tours:**

AeroFarms offers tours of their facilities to local schools, community groups, and the general public to educate them about sustainable farming practices and the benefits of vertical farming.

**Educational Partnerships:**

AeroFarms partners with schools and universities to integrate vertical farming into their curricula. They provide resources and support for creating educational programs that teach students about urban agriculture, sustainability, and healthy eating.

**Youth Programs:**

AeroFarms collaborates with organizations like the Boys and Girls Clubs to create youth programs focused on nutrition education, urban farming, and environmental stewardship. These programs often include hands-on activities such as growing their own crops.

## Regional Highlights

**Asia:** Countries like Japan, Singapore, and China are leading in vertical farming adoption due to high urbanization rates and technological advancement. Japan’s vertical farming industry is particularly advanced, with numerous high-tech farms producing a variety of crops.

**Europe:** The European market is expanding rapidly, with countries like the Netherlands, Germany, and the UK investing heavily in vertical farming technologies. These farms are often integrated into urban settings, contributing to local food security and sustainability.

**North America:** The United States and Canada are seeing a surge in vertical farming startups. These initiatives are supported by robust venture capital investment and a growing demand for locally sourced, sustainable produce.

**Middle East**: With harsh climatic conditions and limited arable land, countries like the UAE and Saudi Arabia are turning to vertical farming to ensure food security. Advanced technologies and significant government support are driving this growth.

**Africa:** Vertical farming is emerging as a promising solution to food security issues in African cities. Innovations are tailored to local conditions, focusing on affordability and resilience.

## Leading Initiatives:

Several high-profile initiatives are spearheading the vertical farming revolution. From state-of-the-art facilities to community-driven projects, these endeavours are reshaping the world’s agricultural landscape.

### AeroFarms

Based in Newark, New Jersey, AeroFarms is a leader in indoor vertical farming, utilizing aeroponic technology to grow leafy greens and herbs with minimal environmental impact.

### [Plenty](https://www.plenty.ag/)

Located in South San Francisco, Plenty focuses on producing pesticide-free, high-quality produce through advanced vertical farming techniques, aiming to provide fresh food to urban populations.

### [Emirates Flight Catering – Dubai](https://www.emiratesflightcatering.com/)

One notable initiative is the Emirates Flight Catering Vertical Farm, a joint venture between Emirates Flight Catering and Crop One Holdings. This massive vertical farm, located near Dubai International Airport, produces a variety of fresh herbs and leafy greens for inflight meals and local consumption. By utilizing vertical farming techniques, the facility ensures a consistent supply of high-quality produce year-round, reducing dependence on imported goods.

### [Badia Farms](https://www.badiafarms.com/)

Another pioneering project is Badia Farms, the region’s first commercial vertical farm. Situated in the heart of Dubai, this innovative farm utilizes hydroponic systems to grow a wide range of crops in a controlled environment. By integrating cutting-edge technology with sustainable practices, Badia Farms demonstrates the potential of vertical farming to transform urban agriculture.

***Expo:*** The expo will showcase the latest products and services in the vertical farming industry. Exhibitors will include technology providers, equipment manufacturers, and service companies. Attendees can explore cutting-edge solutions, witness live demonstrations, and connect with potential partners and suppliers.

## Future Prospects Global Expansion:

Vertical farming is set to expand globally, particularly in regions facing food security challenges and limited arable land. Emerging markets in Asia, the Middle East, and Africa are poised for significant growth.

**Technological Advancements**: Continued innovation in biotechnology, materials science, and renewable energy will enhance the efficiency and scalability of vertical farms.

**Economic Viability**: As technology costs decrease and operational efficiencies improve, vertical farming will become more economically competitive with traditional agriculture.

**Integration with Smart Cities**: Future urban planning may integrate vertical farms into building designs and public spaces, creating green, food-producing infrastructures within cities.

**Policy and Regulation:** Supportive policies and regulations will play a crucial role in the growth of vertical farming. Governments may implement incentives for sustainable farming practices and urban agriculture development.

## Conclusion

Global vertical farming represents a transformative shift in how we produce food, addressing the dual challenges of urbanization and sustainability. By utilizing cutting-edge technology and innovative practices, vertical farms are paving the way for a resilient and sustainable food future. As this field continues to grow and evolve, it holds the promise of making fresh, local produce accessible to urban populations worldwide, ultimately contributing to a healthier planet and society.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Vertical farming is increasingly adopted in urban environments, allowing cities to produce fresh food locally, reduce transportation costs, and address food deserts effectively.

Innovations such as AI, hydroponics, and automated lighting systems enhance vertical farming efficiency, enabling year-round crop production while optimizing resource use and minimizing environmental impact.
Vertical farming promotes sustainability by using less water, eliminating pesticides, and reducing carbon footprints, thus aligning with global environmental goals and enhancing food security.
